channel.title: NYT > Arts > Music
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Gorillaz on ‘The Mountain’ and Celebrating Lost Musicians
item.description: “We need young artists to engage with other things than fame and image and likes,” Damon Albarn said. The band’s latest album proves the point.
item.dc:creator: Brian Raftery
item.media:credit: OK McCausland for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jamie Hewlett and Damon Albarn’s latest Gorillaz album features voices of lost former collaborators.

item.title: Kacey Musgraves, the Pussycat Dolls: 8 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Kacey Musgraves laments her frustration, the Pussycat Dolls attempt a comeback and Bella Kay cracks the Top 40 with a tune about turmoil.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Seth Herald/Reuters
item.media:description: Kacey Musgraves announced a new album, “Middle of Nowhere,” with a song filled with not-so-subtle references.

item.title: Kneecap Gets Serious on Its New Album, ‘Fenian’
item.description: The gonzo Irish M.C.s first gained notice for their playful antics. After attracting global attention for their pro-Palestinian views, their new LP is more serious.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Max Miechowski
item.media:credit: Max Miechowski for The New York Times

item.title: Crisis Follows Conductor’s Dismissal at the Boston Symphony
item.description: A decision by the orchestra’s board to fire its music director, Andris Nelsons, has set off protests from players, leaving their leaders scrambling.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney

item.title: Coalition of States Will Carry on Live Nation Antitrust Case
item.description: The federal government has settled its claims, but dozens of states on Monday will resume a trial that accuses the company of being a monopoly.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Live Nation reached a settlement with the Justice Department that did not satisfy the attorneys general of many states.

item.title: Jack Harlow Was a Chart-Topping Rapper. He Doesn’t Want to Brag Anymore.
item.description: After smashes like “First Class” and “Lovin on Me,” the artist from Louisville, Ky., is making a true musical shift to intimate, hand-played R&B on his fourth album, “Monica.”
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli, Jon Caramanica and Lucia Bell-Epstein For The New York Times
item.media:credit: Lucia Bell-Epstein for The New York Times

item.title: 1,865 Coats in 15 Minutes: A Fine-Tuned Opera House Team
item.description: Each night, the coat check at the Deutsche Oper in Berlin deals with hundreds of jackets, no matter how fancy the audience dresses.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze and Gordon Welters
item.media:credit: Gordon Welters for The New York Times

item.title: When It Came to Their Vows, Rapper Remy Banks and Ashley Condina Didn’t Miss a Beat
item.description: At their Valentine’s Day wedding, Mr. Banks read the verses of a song he wrote for his girlfriend of about six years, the artist Ashley Condina.
item.dc:creator: Tammy LaGorce

item.title: Roscoe Robinson, Gospel Star Who Minted 1960s R&B Hits, Dies at 97
item.description: Although he wasn’t blind, he sang in three different gospel groups known as the Blind Boys before making a splash on the R&B and pop charts.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.category: Five Blind Boys of Mississippi (Music Group)
item.media:credit: via Michael Ochs Archives/Getty Images
item.media:description: Roscoe Robinson in an undated photo. The singer Sam Cooke was an early admirer and helped jump-start his career, telling one gospel group, “’Scoe is the guy you need.”

item.title: Pussycat Dolls Announce New Single and Reunion Tour
item.description: You’re in luck: The early 2000s girl group announced a new single and a reunion tour on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Vince Bucci/Getty Images for Maui and Sons
item.media:description: Nicole Scherzinger, center, and the Pussycat Dolls performing in 2008.

item.title: Live Nation Slacks Reveal Employees Joking About ‘Stupid’ Fans
item.description: A series of private exchanges in the messaging system Slack were revealed as exhibits in the Justice Department’s antitrust lawsuit against the concert giant.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: Live Nation had argued that the exchanges should be excluded from the evidence in the trial, saying they were “irrelevant” private remarks.

item.title: 5 Classical Music Albums You Can Listen to Right Now
item.description: Lise Davidsen’s recital at the Met Opera, Gustavo Dudamel leading “Romeo and Juliet,” and a violinist’s personal program are among our selections.

item.title: On the Hunt for 24 Hours of Drag in N.Y.C.
item.description: An enthusiast went on a madcap adventure to find some fun events for all types across the city, from bingo fans to “Star Trek” aficionados.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg and Amy Lombard
item.media:credit: Amy Lombard for The New York Times

item.title: The Secret History of Rock’s Wildest Stage Prop: P-Funk’s Mothership
item.description: A half-century ago, George Clinton debuted a $500,000 spaceship that turned his live concerts into intergalactic journeys. Here’s how it went down.
item.dc:creator: Steve Knopper
item.media:credit: David Corio/Redferns, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Clinton said he told his label boss, “Get the Mothership,” and that he replied, “Oh, hell yeah.”

item.title: To Pay the Bills, a Cult Band Becomes a Doors Tribute Act
item.description: With even critically acclaimed bands struggling to make a living, the British group Field Music has come up with a novel way to top up its income.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Joanna Yee
item.media:credit: Joanna Yee for The New York Times

item.title: Suspect in Rihanna Shooting Is Charged With Attempted Murder
item.description: A woman fired a semiautomatic weapon at Rihanna’s home while she was there with ASAP Rocky and their three children on Sunday, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Charly Triballeau/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Nobody was injured in the shooting at Rihanna’s Los Angeles mansion on Sunday.

item.title: How ‘Sync Music’ Became the Soundtrack to Our Lives
item.description: “Sync music” has become the soundtrack to our lives — whether we realize it or not.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Francis Bradley
item.media:credit: Emily Shur for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kara Talve (left) and Anze Rozman, who are married to each other, collaborating on a composition at Bleeding Fingers studios in Santa Monica, Calif.

item.title: Harry Styles and 8 More Boy Band Stars Who Went Solo
item.description: As the former One Direction singer releases his fourth album, listen back to how Bobby Brown, Ricky Martin and others made their own statements.
item.media:credit: Gareth Cattermole, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Harry Styles onstage at the Brit Awards earlier this month.

item.title: Dozens of States Weigh Continuing Live Nation Antitrust Case
item.description: After the Justice Department reached a settlement with the concert giant, the judge overseeing the trial asked the states and the company to do the same.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Justice Department, joined by 39 states and the District of Columbia, sued Live Nation in May 2024, arguing that the company was a monopoly that dominated the music industry.

item.title: Review: A New ‘Tristan und Isolde’ at the Metropolitan Opera
item.description: Yuval Sharon’s debut production featured an astonishing performance by the soprano Lise Davidsen. It is the event of the season.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael Spyres, left, and Lise Davidsen in the tunnel as the title lovers in “Tristan und Isolde” at the Metropolitan Opera.

item.title: New York Philharmonic Announces 2026-27 Season
item.description: A new era for the orchestra begins in September, when Gustavo Dudamel officially becomes its music and artistic director.
item.media:credit: James Estrin/The New York Times
item.media:description: Gustavo Dudamel, who will officially become the New York Philharmonic’s music and artistic director in the 2026-27 season.

item.title: At 100, György Kurtág Is Still Searching for the Right Note
item.description: György Kurtág, the eminent composer and Hungarian national treasure, has reached a rare milestone yet shows little interest in retirement.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Akos Stiller for The New York Times
item.media:description: The composer György Kurtág in 2018. He recently celebrated his 100th birthday in Budapest.

item.title: After Kennedy Center Exit, Washington National Opera Returns
item.description: Washington National Opera managed to resume performances within two months of its abrupt departure. But there are still challenges ahead.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Elman Studio
item.media:description: Washington National Opera’s new production of Scott Joplin’s “Treemonisha,” which opened at Lisner Auditorium at George Washington University on Saturday.

item.title: Review: Harry Styles Hits the Club on ‘Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally.’
item.description: The pop star’s new album revels in the communal experience of clubbing. But his gleaming songs don’t reveal much about the man behind them.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.category: Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally. (Album)
item.media:credit: Gareth Cattermole/Getty Images
item.media:description: “Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally.” is the fourth solo album by the former One Direction member Harry Styles.

item.title: Live Nation, Ticketmaster’s Owner, Settles Antitrust Case With Justice Dept.
item.description: The agreement, which requires a judicial sign-off, would avoid a breakup of the concert giant. But states that joined the suit object to the terms.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario, David McCabe and Olivia Bensimon
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The tentative settlement ends a case that had bipartisan political support in Washington.

item.title: After a Shooting at Rihanna’s Home, a Woman Is Held on Suspicion of Attempted Murder
item.description: No injuries were reported after the shooting on Sunday. A 35-year-old woman is being held on roughly $10 million bail, jail records show.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens and Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Mike Coppola/Getty Images For The Gotham Film
item.media:description: Rihanna has three children with her partner, ASAP Rocky.

item.title: Country Joe McDonald, Whose Antiwar Song Became an Anthem, Dies at 84
item.description: One of the starring acts at Woodstock, he and his band, the Fish, came out of the Bay Area’s psychedelic rock scene. He went on to a long career as a solo artist.
item.dc:creator: Jim Farber
item.category: Country Joe and the Fish (Music Group)
item.media:credit: United Archives, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Country Joe McDonald in 1981. The tone of the politics and social commentary in his songs could range from whimsical to snarky.
